sourceTitle: This project management system helps your engineering team track every initiative

questions.question: Why does Notion recommend using a single board as both a roadmap and a project‑management system for engineering teams?

options.text: It forces every project to follow the same workflow, eliminating flexibility.
options.explanation: The board is customizable; it doesn’t force a single workflow, it just provides a central place to view and move projects.

options.text: It keeps all project information, tasks, and status updates in one place, reducing context‑switching.
options.explanation: A single board lets engineers see the roadmap, related tasks, and documentation together, saving time.

options.text: It automatically generates code for each task.
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t auto‑generate code; it provides a place to embed code snippets.

options.text: It replaces the need for any other engineering tools like GitHub or Figma.
options.explanation: Notion integrates with other tools but doesn’t replace them entirely.

questions.question: Which property type should you use to tag an engineer as the owner of an epic?

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select creates dropdown tags, not person assignments.

options.text: Person
options.explanation: The Person property lets you assign a Notion user as the owner.

options.text: Date
options.explanation: Date records a calendar date, not a user.

options.text: Formula
options.explanation: Formula calculates values; it can’t directly assign a user.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using the 'Relation' property between epics and tasks?

options.text: To automatically copy task descriptions into the epic page.
options.explanation: Relation links records; it doesn’t duplicate content.

options.text: To display all tasks that belong to an epic within the epic’s page.
options.explanation: Relation lets you view related tasks directly on the epic card.

options.text: To enforce a strict hierarchy that cannot be edited.
options.explanation: Relations are flexible and can be edited or removed.

options.text: To change the color of task cards based on the epic.
options.explanation: Color coding is done via Select or Multi‑select properties, not Relation.

questions.question: If you want to view only the bugs scheduled to ship this week, which combination of features would you use?

options.text: Create a Table view, filter where Type = Bug and Ship Date is within the next 7 days.
options.explanation: Filtering by the Type select and a Date property isolates the desired bugs.

options.text: Group the board by the 'Priority' property and look at the top column.
options.explanation: Grouping shows categories, not a date‑based filter.

options.text: Add a Multi‑select tag called 'This Week' to each bug manually.
options.explanation: Manual tagging works but is less dynamic than using a date filter.

options.text: Use the 'Sort' feature to sort by Ship Date ascending and scroll to the top.
options.explanation: Sorting orders items but doesn’t hide non‑bug items.

questions.question: How can you give a sub‑team (e.g., infrastructure) a focused view of only its epics while preserving overall transparency?

options.text: Create a separate Notion workspace for the sub‑team.
options.explanation: A separate workspace isolates the team completely, losing shared visibility.

options.text: Create a linked database view filtered to the sub‑team’s engineer Person property.
options.explanation: Linked views let you filter by engineer while keeping the master board visible to all.

options.text: Duplicate the entire board and delete unrelated epics.
options.explanation: Duplication creates data silos and can cause inconsistencies.

options.text: Hide the master board and only show the sub‑team’s board.
options.explanation: Hiding the master board defeats the purpose of company‑wide transparency.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes why templates are valuable for engineering processes?

options.text: They force every engineer to use the exact same code.
options.explanation: Templates standardize documentation, not code.

options.text: They provide a consistent structure for recurring tasks, saving time and building institutional knowledge.
options.explanation: Templates give engineers a repeatable format, ensuring key information is captured.

options.text: They automatically assign tasks to the highest‑priority engineer.
options.explanation: Assignment still requires manual selection or automation outside of templates.

options.text: They replace the need for meeting notes.
options.explanation: Meeting notes remain a separate record; templates supplement but don’t replace them.

questions.question: What advantage does embedding a code snippet (using /code) inside a project page provide?

options.text: It automatically runs the code in Notion’s environment.
options.explanation: Notion displays code but does not execute it.

options.text: It lets engineers view and discuss the exact code relevant to a project without leaving Notion.
options.explanation: Embedding keeps the code visible for review and feedback within the same page.

options.text: It syncs the snippet with the repository in real time.
options.explanation: Syncing requires external integrations; Notion only stores a static copy.

options.text: It converts the snippet into a downloadable binary.
options.explanation: Notion does not compile or package code.

questions.question: When configuring the board’s columns (groups), which property types can be used to group items?

options.text: Only the built‑in Status property.
options.explanation: You can group by any Select, Multi‑select, or Person property, not just Status.

options.text: Any property that has a dropdown list, such as Select, Multi‑select, or Person.
options.explanation: These property types provide discrete values that the board can group by.

options.text: Date and Formula properties.
options.explanation: Date and Formula cannot be used directly for grouping columns.

options.text: Only properties that are marked as 'Show in board view'.
options.explanation: Visibility is separate from grouping capability.

questions.question: Which best practice ensures clear communication about who to contact for a specific epic?

options.text: Add a single Person property named 'Owner' to the epic.
options.explanation: Having only one owner may hide the broader team responsible for execution.

options.text: Create two Person properties: one for the project owner and one for the execution team.
options.explanation: Distinguishing ownership and execution clarifies responsibilities and who to ask questions.

options.text: Use a Select property with the names of all engineers on the epic.
options.explanation: Select is not ideal for assigning people; Person properties provide proper user linking.

options.text: Rely on the @mention feature in comments instead of properties.
options.explanation: Mentions are transient; explicit Person properties give a permanent record.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to link meeting notes to epics using a Relation property?

options.text: So meeting notes automatically become tasks in the epic.
options.explanation: Relation links records; it doesn’t convert notes into tasks.

options.text: To keep a complete, searchable history of discussions alongside the work they affect, improving asynchronous transparency.
options.explanation: Linking notes provides context and ensures anyone can see decisions even if they missed the meeting.

options.text: Because it forces all engineers to attend every meeting.
options.explanation: Linking notes doesn’t affect attendance; it merely records the outcome.

options.text: To enable Notion to send automatic email reminders about upcoming meetings.
options.explanation: Email reminders are not a function of Relation properties.
